comments.title: Thoughts on Shadow Tech and Question on Occultism Resource "Wishlist"
comments.content:
Good Evening,

To all here, may the tonight's full moon bring fulfillment and maximization of what you'd have, and not of what you wouldn't!

To Share: I've written up my thoughts on another book on coming to know your Shadow and how to work with what is within it. As is often the case with me, I found it helpful, but wished for more "hands-on" advice: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_thoughts_on_shadow_tech

To Ask: JMG, and others here, what kinds of resources do you wish there were or were more of in the occultism/alternative spirituality/magical world? Book topics, correspondence courses, periodicals, newsletters, services, whatever else? As I continue my work, I'm starting to look for ways to do worthwhile things for other folks, so I figured I'd start here and get an idea what folks actually want, so I can start building up the ability to deliver some of that.

Thank you again, JMG and everyone here!

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Shadow Tech and Question on Occultism Resource "Wishlist"
replies.content:
Hmm. For the American occult community, I wish there were more books translated from other languages -- the English-speaking occult tradition is a very small fraction of what's out there in the world of magic, and since it's probably a lost cause to get Americans to learn to read any other language, translations would help.

I wish there were more ways for information on occult classes, lectures, and instructional opportunities to get out to interested people. Saturday I taught a class in Salem, MA on the first level of the Modern Order of Essenes healing work; it went well and all that, but I've already heard from several people who'd have liked to be there but didn't know it was happening.

At some point it would be good to have a few more self-supporting venues for occult instruction, along the lines of Manly P. Hall's Philosophical Research Society, which has been offering talks and classes on occultism since the 1930s. That would take some serious fundraising but I don't think it's out of reach.

Those are the ideas that come first to mind.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Shadow Tech and Question on Occultism Resource "Wishlist"
replies.content: There's an astounding amount of important stuff that's never been translated from French into English. None of the works of Stanislaus de Guaita, among the most influential of Lévi's early heirs, have been translated. Only one of Joséphin Péladan's books has yet been put in English -- admittedly it's his most important work, How to Become a Mage, but it would be worth translating his most famous novel, The Supreme Vice, and some of his other magical works are valuable. Most 19th and 20th century French alchemical works remain untranslated. Fortunately Piers Vaughan has turned most of the important works on Martinism into English -- that wasn't true until quite recently -- but most of the world of creative occult ventures that Tobias Churton chronicled in Occult Paris remains unknown to Anglophone readers. How's that for a start?

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Shadow Tech and Question on Occultism Resource "Wishlist"
replies.content:
I don't have any experience with this, but a few thoughts that come to mind:

1) Finding "adjacent markets" to use a bit of business-speak is likely a good way to start, especially if looking to avoid social media, which is just about the only way anyone tries to find potential "customers" these days. Funnily enough, I hit on the idea recently to start a Germanic mythology reading group in the new year.

2) On how to keep a reading library from losing its books: two immediate thoughts that come to mind are a. not to be a lending library, so anyone who wants to read the books there has to do so on the premises (of course, for that you have to have premises), or b. require a deposit for any books taken out. There are likely clever ways to structure this latter option besides giving the deposit back when the book comes back. Maybe you have a monthly/annual membership fee that comes with a set number of books out at a time, or you roll over any deposits to new books if requested, or something like that. Maybe combine lending with a small amount of selling, using profits to replenish/expand the library.

3) That seems like a good list of reading material to begin with, but is very beginner-focused. Certainly that makes sense when the library is new, and to keep up with as it gets established, but assuming you develop a longer-lasting membership, having some more intermediate and advanced works would possibly be useful, especially if the library is at all meant to be linked with any working groups (one example: you found a lodge of the Order of the Golden Section and stock the library with a few copies of the whole course of books for it, so that folks can join in the work more readily). Personally, I find myself wishing for access to expensive or hard to find academic books on mythology, but the trouble there is that without a lot of work and money, you either end up with a very specialized, narrow-scoped library, or one that's not much deeper in any one subject than what someone could buy for himself.

Anyhow, not sure how helpful this is, but thought I'd say thanks for sharing your ideas by bouncing a few back.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Five Fortune Cookies
comments.content:
A journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step. I have never had that golden nugget as a fortune cookie 'fortune' but I think it qualifies. Maybe I'll put it on my tombstone.

For most of this year, I have been slowly reading my way through the Occult Philosophy Workbook. It's been a pleasure and between what I glean from the lessons and many of the MM comments, I have found that the occult path is definitely a thousand mile journey, a journey that I am only a few miles in. Please consider this a report card of sorts as framed around a set of fortune cookies I recently came across. The first three came on a night I really didn't want Chinese take-out for dinner, my only consolation was a fist-full of fortune cookies, and as I ate them all, I put each fortune aside as I often do. One in particular, I thought appropriate to comment upon here but all three stood out in their own way. In my typical way of saving them, I ended up washing them all by accident. Upset with myself, I tried to recall each as best I could. Two were easy but the third eluded me until my youngest child's laughter brought it rushing back.

May the laughter of the day echo through your heart.

I knew then, why I liked that fortune. It applied succinctly to my life. The other, more easy to remember cookie's fortune, told me another, at least applied to me, self-evident truth.

November is a good month to focus on your goals.

And indeed it was. I felt a clarity about the path that for most of the year prior, if not longer, eluded me. I realized that my recent, nearly five years now, stint without alcohol wouldn't be the ten years I had planned it to be. I realized that going back to drinking wouldn't be getting out of prison, it would be going back in. The spiritual path that I am making baby steps towards demands that. It must be for the rest of this short lifetime. To be honest, maybe even at that, it may not be enough time, I'm fifty plus two, very soon to be three, moving very slowly with my spiritual pursuits, but in my November clarity, came purpose, drive and determination to over come my real Mt. Everest, my licentiousness. It's my eight-hundred pound gorilla that's always busy rubbing himself in the corner. I've read and re-read the post on the metaphysics of sex, I've read all the posts on will and tried the exercises and throughout, it's been a tale of moderate success always followed by failure. I came to a point earlier this year, that I thought it smarter to just accept my flaw and stop beating myself up about it, pun intended. But even at that, it wouldn't do, I couldn't accept it. In November I had simply added prayer to my meditation sessions that I do at the end of each day. November was a smashing success. With the second or third of December, came a crushing defeat. Nothing unusual in the struggle but painful as it really felt like one giant step forward only to have two, just as giant steps back, all of which brings me to the strangest fortune cookie 'fortunes' I have ever received. It went something like this.

You should communicate with other likeminded individuals going through your same mental struggles.

I had to laugh and then I had laugh again. I have no friends, at least not any I keep in good contact with, I hardly talk to family. My big brother died suddenly in '22 and in many ways, I'm still reeling from the shock. There has been nothing more painful than losing the one person in this world that I could lay bare to, all the struggles of being a middle-aged married man. And at my closest points, I have two young children who I adore and a stressed out wife I could make love to every night but never do. Silence is golden and it is my thread to survival through these trying times. The best silence is the moments before bed that I practice discursive meditation and now finish with prayer. Maybe going too hard with prayer in November led to the inevitable backlash. It's too soon to know but I have tempered it. And with that, I'm coming to a semi-balance that is a real effort to workout, which brings me to the last two fortune cookies. The first's message was simple yet not.

Establish harmony and balance in your life.

Yeah, working on it, fates! I took that in and then promptly recycled that strip of paper. The next and last fortune is the one I have in front of me as I write this, that I will keep in mind as I live out the last years of this life trying to pursue something I know is difficult.

Adversity is the first path to truth.

And that is it, that is my reality, my report card. I'd give me an F+, or at best, a D- but am curious to your thoughts. This going down the spiritual path is not easy nor for the faint of heart. I'm at Lesson 34 and plugging along and as always, grateful for all you do here.

All the best,
Mint Nauseous Piglet

replies.title: Re: Five Fortune Cookies
replies.content: I don't really have any thoughts on your experiences (and fortune cookie oracles), other than to acknowledge that the path of occultism involves a lot of work and sometimes a lot of heartache. Oh, and I don't recommend grading yourself. One of the things that regular practice will teach you over time is that you can never be an objective observer of how well you're doing at any stage of the work; we all either overestimate or underestimate how well we've done. Keep at it, and let the road teach you how to walk it.

1. Are you aware of a kind of venue you could find in any mid-size city that would cheaply rent or freely offer space appropriate for individual practice of ceremonial magic on a short-term, even hourly, basis?
2. I'm confused about odor in magic. In ancient Egypt, I've heard that people were barred from entering temples if they had eaten fish or garlic. A friend who practices Hindu tantra says that it's important to take a shower before doing practices from those traditions. The Picatrix insists that the house you invoke perfect nature in be very clean. Yet I haven't heard much about this with respect to Golden Dawn magic and it seems to be most heavily emphasized in pre-modern traditions. Why is that? My thinking so far is that these kinds of odors are disruptive to practices aimed at generating dense, almost material effluvia, yet presumably that would be important with some kinds of Golden Dawn magic, too.

1) No, I'm not. It's an interesting question, though. Anyone else?

2) Different systems of practice have different requirements. One size emphatically does not fit all! That's why it's important to pay attention to the traditional requirements of whatever system you practice, and remember that rules from other, unrelated traditions do not apply.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
On 1., the two options that come to mind are certain motels and shared workspaces. Of course, the kinds of motels that will rent out hourly usually cater to, shall we say, less spiritually-inclined pursuits, so you might run into some issues with the broader issues of spiritual crud your question in 2. alludes to.

For workspaces, I'm not sure how common it is to offer hourly/as-needed space without a regular membership, but the usual set-up for individuals or small businesses is that you rent out some set space, or pay a membership to have access to non-private space (something like, there's a room full of tables/booths, and if you're a member, you can come in and use those whenever you want. There's usually coffee or the like available as well), but such spaces also usually have conference rooms. The plus side is such rooms have doors that can be closed and are usually reasonably insulated for sound, the downsides are that they usually have a big table in the middle and modern office style tends to favor lots of windows, so privacy might be wanting for magical rituals. Also, since such spaces are intended for folks who are at least trying to make money at what they're doing there, they might not be the cheapest option available. Still, it might be worth checking out.

Another option that would not be on a daily/hourly basis, but might be affordable enough to consider is self-storage. Such units tend to be air-conditioned, the walls and doors are corrugated steel, so privacy is guaranteed, they only have what you put in there, so you can have as much open space as you can afford/want, and you can come and go as you like during regular business hours.

Hope these give some helpful ideas, and good luck!
Jeff

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good morning and thank you for hosting.

Some spiritual traditions, particularly the sort of watered down eastern philosophies that have trickled over to the west in incomplete form, seem to treat the ego as something to be dissolved and rejected. What is the place of the ego, in contrast, in the hermetic/broadly western traditions?

I’ve never entirely understood what its purpose is supposed to be and what it looks like in healthy balance—I’ve seen it described as the part of us that keeps the overall self alive by defining the boundary between self and other, but plenty of creatures with considerably less behavioral complexity than humans seem perfectly capable of self preservation without seeming to have anything resembling an ego.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Your ego is simply your self-representation -- your own notion of who and what you are. That's all it is. It can be problematic but it doesn't have to be, and I'm far from sure I believe that it's possible to get rid of it. Can you repress it? Sure, but then you get the inevitable return of the repressed in unconscious form; it's probably not an accident that everyone I've ever met who claims to have transcended the ego was a monstrous egomaniac. The No-Ego Ego Trip is extremely common in modern American pop spirituality!

As I see it, the ego becomes problematic when it's out of step with reality. If who and what you think you are doesn't match who and what you actually are, you end up constantly doing battle on behalf of a self-image you know to be false. That leads to all the usual pathologies of hypocrisy, bullying, and the rest of it. The way to solve that isn't to repress the ego -- that just makes all those pathologies even more unconscious than they were; it's to follow the advice of the Delphic Oracle, Gnothi Se Auton, "Know Thyself." As your self-representation becomes more and more a simple reflection of who you actually are, the pathologies of ego go away, and you're left with a clear and useful sense of who you are and what your strengths and limits are.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks for your definition of ego! I remember a video about an old farm worker who was evicted in the 1950s. He saw himself as part of the farm, not as an individual. The interviewer asked personal questions but the worker never replied with "I". His ego did not seem repressed but not developed at all.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I think your ego gets naturally put aside when times demand that you put others above yourself consistently and this makes us humble and good. But it's just a different mode of operating from and when time is kinder and you return to self care you incorporate that experience into your ego and might soften but you can focus on your own self identity again.

replies.title: Re: Profections
replies.content: The Hellenistic astrology revival sparked by Project Hindsight, and then popularized by Chris Brennan, has brought Annual Profections back into popular usage. It is a lovely and simple technique, and in Vedic astrology we have the Sudarshana Chakra Dasha, which is basically the same technique, though made slightly more complex (we go from the Sun and Moon as well as Ascendant instead of just the Ascendant). It's a good tool to have in the toolbox, another important layer to give a complete picture and help with predictions. It's best to be used with other timing techniques though, like Zodiacal Releasing as well as transits in a Hellenistic framework, or with Vimshottari and other dashas (and transits) in Jyotish.

replies.title: Re: Profections
replies.content:
"Profection" is simply an archaic term for primary directions. Those, along with most other predictive techniques, dropped out of use in popular American astrology in the mid-20th century, when the purely psychological approach of Marc Edmund Jones and Dane Rudhyar became fashionable; now it's come back into use via the current vogue for Hellenistic astrology. Myself, I find progressions (aka secondary directions) more useful for annual predictions, but your mileage may vary.

As for the archetype in question, that always becomes popular when the rich and powerful treat the rest of the population as a resource to be plundered and disposed of at whim. That's a very popular attitude among the obscenely rich these days; you might look into the cult status of Bonnie and Clyde back in the 1930s, when banks and bankers had richly earned the same sort of unpopularity that health insurance companies have earned more recently.

comments.title: Theodicy examples?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

It is my general understanding that the Book of Job and the story of the Garden of Eden are two examples of theodicy.

replies.title: Re: Theodicy examples?
replies.content:
1) Theodicy is only an issue in religions that insist that there's only one god, who is omnipotent, omniscient, and omnibenevolent; given that claim, explaining why life sucks as much as it does is very, very hard to defend. In polytheist religions -- the Druidry I practice is one of these -- that's not an issue, because we recognize that the gods are many, and they're superhuman but not omni-anything. My book A World Full of Gods covers this in quite some detail.

2) I don't know of any -- again, it's not an issue outside of the Abrahamic faiths.

3) No, not at all. Anything that's subject to human choice is positive evil, and the choice to drive while intoxicated is included in that. Negative evil is the basic cussedness of existence, which has nothing to do with individual choice -- for example, the realities of old age and death.

replies.title: Re: Theodicy examples?
replies.content: One of the many ways in which polytheism has enriched my own worship of the Abrahamic god has been the elimination of any pressing need for a theodicy.  I've had a few interesting (really, entertaining) discussions with skeptics and atheists who are just flabbergasted that their usual critiques of theism fail to do much damage to polytheistic views.  Anyway, about the only plausible theodicy I can think of for a monotheist would be reincarnation/karma with some kind of apokatastasis as the eschaton. replies.title: Re: Theodicy examples?
replies.content: The story of Job is probably a theodicy but the Garden of Eden story probably originated during a time when the ancient Israelites were still polytheistic (though maybe henotheistic by that point), and so is more properly an etiological account of the origins of death, marriage, child birth, agricultural labor, etc.  Other polytheistic cultures, especially in the ancient Near East, had their own versions of that.  Reading a theodicy into it is a later development, once monotheism was finally emerging within the Hebrew and later Christian traditions.

replies.title: Re: Theodicy examples?
replies.content:
In at least one brand of polytheism, the Gods ARE omnipresent, omnipotent, and omniscient, because these terms are not referenced in relation to contingency. As each God is with each God in a non-physical existence, each God knows each God, is present to each God, and can do what any other God is capable of in terms of choosing Their relations amongst Themselves or in bringing forth what is in some other God in a way that is in keeping with the character of the choosing God.

Theodicy in this system unrolls from the fact that each principle that develops from these relations creates a chain of existence where each rank is somewhat less perfect than the one previous to it, as say, one moves from Intellect, then to Life, and on to Being.

One of the modern problems with monotheistic definitions for the omni-traits is that it relegates the existence of a God to being a cause as a first condition, when no cause can be understood apart from it's effect. This means that in monotheism, as God is omnipotent, He can cause any contingent state of affairs, as He is omniscient He fully knows the states of affairs He brings about, and as He is the cause of each contingent state of affairs, He is present to each and every one of them. Now, take out the term contingency from the argument, and the entire system falls down in pieces. This challenges divine simplicity, as God can no longer be said to be understood under His own terms, but is instead only intelligible when we include something that is not necessary, and "not necessary" is practically the definition of contingency.

replies.title: Re: Theodicy examples?
replies.content:
You might be able to staple together something halfway satisfying from the following parts:


- Raymond Smullyan's theodicy, presented in a dialogue titled "Is God a Taoist?":

> GOD:
> [...] According to Orthodox Judaism, I created angels, and they have no free will. They are in actual sight of me and are so completely attracted by goodness that they never have even the slightest temptation toward evil[....] if your rabbi's explanation were correct, why wouldn't I have simply created only angels rather than mortals?
>
> [...] the explanation is simply not correct. In the first place, I have never created any ready-made angels. All sentient beings ultimately approach the state which might be called "angelhood." But just as the race of human beings is in a certain stage of biologic evolution, so angels are simply the end result of a process of Cosmic Evolution. The only difference between the so-called saint and the so-called sinner is that the former is vastly older than the latter. Unfortunately it takes countless life cycles to learn what is perhaps the most important fact of the universe -- evil is simply painful. All the arguments of the moralists -- all the alleged reasons why people shouldn't commit evil acts -- simply pale into insignificance in light of the one basic truth that evil is suffering.
>
> [...] Those who wish to think of the devil (although I wish they wouldn't!) might analogously define him as the unfortunate length of time the process takes. In this sense, the devil is necessary; the process simply does take an enormous length of time, and there is absolutely nothing I can do about it. But, I assure you, once the process is more correctly understood, the painful length of time will no longer be regarded as an essential limitation or an evil. It will be seen to be the very essence of the process itself. I know this is not completely consoling to you who are now in the finite sea of suffering, but the amazing thing is that once you grasp this fundamental attitude, your very finite suffering will begin to diminish -- ultimately to the vanishing point.

- The concept of "computational irreducibility" and Jonathan Gorard's "slowdown theorem", trying to relate them to the length of the process that Smullyan describes. Gorard's theorem was published in 2018, but it used a Gödelian argument, and Smullyan was a logician who would have been familiar with that kind of argument, so he might have had intuitions for how a similar argument might be used to show that it was impossible to accelerate this kind of evolution from the outside beyond a certain point.

- A cosmology one or two steps removed from the Mormon Transhumanists. Mormonism has a cosmology with this kind of evolution in it, and the transhumanist wing can help with conceptual bridges to the other stuff.

- The kind of ideas about smaller and larger scales of organization that were popularly presented in the book "Nonzero: The Logic of Human Cooperation". Smullyan's dialogue doesn't directly explain some importantly relevant points about the lack of inherent distinctions privileging the individual-organism level of organization. Maybe karma (and also incarnation itself?) might just be a way of intervening at the individual level to accelerate certain updating dynamics that would eventually have happened anyway much more slowly at the level of collectives, which is closely related to the themes of "Nonzero".

- And I'd throw in the concepts of "moral hazard" or "due process" with respect to the ways that the entities managing the karma process would have to be careful not to put their thumbs on the scales and fool themselves as to what "good" meant. One thing related to this that is online is the fictional "Toposophics Diversification Conjectures", https://www.orionsarm.com/eg-article/47fae26892bea which is a plausibly-worldbuilt example of how higher powers might regard lower powers as important not to bruise the independent development of.

Like, the theodicy might partly explain the motivation for why a karma system even exists at all, and why it would have the limitations that it did.

However, I don't know anyone to have developed this position further, online or off.

Also you should read about Robert Trivers's hypothesis about adaptive self-deception, which is basically the thing in the materialist worldview that plays the role of original sin in the Christian worldview. It's not like it's wrong, at least as far as it goes.

comments.title: Finding purpose/true will
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Can you suggest some techniques or practices, magical or otherwise (my background is Western ceremonial/HD), to find one's purpose?

I do not seem to have a clear idea of what I should be doing, particularly in the career sphere, but more generally I feel like I am just buffeted by fate or circumstance from one event to another without fixed purpose.

Thank you as always for this space and for the opportunity to ask questions,

replies.title: Re: Finding purpose/true will
replies.content:
There's this very widespread belief in today's world that every one of us comes into the world with a preset purpose. Most people end up floundering as a result, because they can't find anything of the kind. I suggest that the reason for this is that the belief is wrong: some people are born with some specific purpose, but most of us don't bring that kind of burden into incarnation with them.

If you don't have a clear sense that you have some specific purpose in existence, odds are that you don't have one. You therefore have a choice. You can simply enjoy existing and let life lead you in whatever directions happen to come up, or you can choose a purpose by the sheer arbitrary exercise of your will, and get out there and make it happen. Both of those are valid choices.

Thus I suggest that the reason you can't find anything that you should be doing is that you can do as you choose. If you want a purpose, you have to provide one for yourself. If you don't, there's no need for you to get one. That is to say, you really are free, and the universe doesn't worry about whether you do this or that.

replies.title: Re: Finding purpose/true will
replies.content: Not the OP. It seems the belief in a preset purpose is a kind of coping mechanism. Today's world is full of different possibilities and paths to walk, but all of them seem to offer nothing but suffering, and then you die. If only there was the right path to take, and everything else was just a result of walking face-first into a wall, it all would be more bearable. Of course, this is the result of one's attitude, which one can (and, probably, should) change, and choosing a purpose is an important part of the process, but then again, with enough willpower it is possible to train yourself to enjoy waterboarding and such, which makes life seem even more cruel: you are here suffer and you better learn to enjoy it.

I think there’s another way to look at this: the sacred time, the turning of the wheel of the year from darker to brighter, the births of various saviors, the enfolding together of the beloved living and the dead during the holy nights of Yuletide, this time of transcendent mystery…all these are celebrated now *because* it is the dark time of the year, the low ebb of the hemisphere’s tides.

The energies of frenzied consumerism are a jarring mismatch and distraction from the deep energies we might otherwise be tuning into. I think it is the mismatch of these energies in our current culture, and the lack of attention and appreciation for the deep mysteries of this tide—which blend dark and light, dearth and fullness, depth and height, immanence and transcendence—that subconsciously pull people into negative states during this time.

If we really have to have a consumerist holiday, it would comport better with the energies of the summer sun-wending, midsummer’s bounty, rather than winter’s call toward the silent, holy nights.

Not that I fail to honor the appropriateness of festive celebration of holy Yuletide, the joyful coming together with those we love; it’s just that the joy goes deeper when we honor the deep, dark roots that underlie the powerful, shifting Earth-tides of Yule.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: The Trump Enigma & Scottish Rite Connection?
replies.content:
Yes, I saw that. There are a lot of 33rd degree Scottish Rite Masons -- every local body of the Scottish Rite elevates somebody to that rank every year or two. Among the 33rd degree Masons I found in a quick search were presidents Harry Truman and Gerald Ford, astronauts John Glenn and Buzz Aldrin, golfer Arnold Palmer, and John Elway, the famous quarterback for the Denver Broncos. I don't imagine you'd consider any of those to be profound mystics or wizards! Norman Vincent Peale was an important writer in the New Thought field, most famous for the book The Power of Positive Thinking; it's a worthwhile book if you've had no previous exposure to the power of mental attitude, but I wouldn't call it profound.

As for the Scottish Rite, it's like the rest of Masonry: there are deep teachings woven into the old rituals, but you have to look long and hard to find anybody in the organization who's even interested in such things. Most Masons treat the Craft as a social club or a venue for charitable work, and go through the rituals by rote. The leadership in most jurisdictions is hostile to anything even remotely close to esotericism. A lot of Scottish Rite Masons are devout, churchgoing Christians who would be horrified if they realized what's actually in the rituals they perform.

I know of a FL Mason who has told me that bringing up anything esoteric in his Lodge would have been grounds for Masonic Charges.

Contrast that with my home state of AZ, where we have a plethora of esoterically minded Masons and regular meetups. Most of the new Seekers we see at my Lodge are coming specifically because they are looking for more esoteric things, and we take strides to encourage them. So all that to say, your mileage may vary. But if you've moved States, it may be worth while to looking into local Lodges and see if one has more of what you are looking for.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There are suggestive facts about the natural history of these materials.

Cocoons are made of silk. The proper organization of the dissolution and rearranging of anatomical structures during metamorphosis might be vulnerable to outside perturbations, like how pregnant women become much more sensitive to random plant chemicals presumably because fetal development is vulnerable to perturbations.

Linen is made of fibers mostly from the hard layer of flax stalks next to the outer shell.

Cotton, conversely, isn't a barrier in its natural form; it's loose and fluffy, just dense enough to keep a wisp of cotton supported internally against being crushed by wind and rain, so that it can catch the wind while carrying seeds.

This leads to the conjectures: Leather might work or mostly work (especially leather from the abdomen near physiologically complex organs like kidneys, liver, endocrine glands, and sometimes fetuses). Wood wouldn't particularly work except perhaps for layers close to the surface. (For crafting purposes consider birch bark. Bark also typically has defensive biochemicals concentrated in it.) It's hard to know what to predict about wool since it's fluffy but also tries to make a barrier against cold air.

Lion mane hair or human beard hair are interesting possibilities, since they are conjectured to have both arisen due to evolutionarily recent increases (too recent for a cost-reducing equilibrium of instincts to develop, like in wolves) in the frequency of potentially hostile contacts between conspecific males with access to stabbing or slashing armaments (in lions due to pack-hunting sociality; in humans due to tool-making). Would a material still block subtle energies if its natural purpose was to impede attack mechanically?

replies.content:
I can dowse but as I'm unsure exactly how I do it teaching is difficult. I've tried in the past without much success, but I'll write again here in hope it is useful.

The main point for me is to get the conscious mind out of the way which can take some practice. The easiest physical method to help is to hold the dowsing object in a way that is counterintuitive to creating the desired movement, aka make the dowsing object difficult to control.

For a rod to find water I use a bent piece of wire (90deg, two legs about 6" each) threaded down through the fingers of my closed fist, other leg pointing forward. The fingers should not directly hold the wire, instead the fingers press against the palm and the wire loose between. The wire then 'flops' around, flicking left or right with little to no movement.

You might want to try the above first as the 'feeling' of loss of control is easier to learn than with a pendulum. I get a slightly panicked sense of not knowing where the wire is going to move and that is a strong indicator of the the correct mindset.

For a pendulum, the cord draped over an extended index finger works well for me (does not matter much which finger). Then set the pendulum swinging and see which way it cycles. What you DO NOT want to do is hold the cord between two (or more) fingers (like you might hold a pen) and swing it - that gives you far too much conscious control.

With any any method one must firmly decide before starting what the questions is and what the movements will mean, and do not amend or alter those questions or meanings whilst in dowsing - thus binary questions are easiest.

When using a wire, that might be a simple 90deg turn (left or right) meaning being above the water pipe (which I find very effective).

For a pendulum, clockwise circles for a Yes, anticlockwise for No is simple. If you don't quickly get a definitive direction then either the question is poorly worded or there is no clear answer.

If there is concern about emotion getting involved and consciously influencing the answer, I'll add another layer of indirection - for example writing the question in positive or negative form on two separate bits of identical paper, turn them over, scramble the location and then do a yes/no on each of those and see if they agree. I might even add some blank pieces to making mixing them up easier and as a good independent check - the blanks will give indeterminate answers.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Quantum computers are not so much "accessing" parallel universes as briefly holding together a tiny sliver of the universes branching from a given point in time, in ways that they are not normally held together.

Normally, different parallel universes would break apart and scatter, like different little bits of a wavefront being broken and sent in different directions by a pattern of variations in seafloor height, in random directions corresponding to different "decohered" measurable outcomes. But if you keep the bits of metaphorical wavefront held in a sort of "channel" alongside each other, you can do computation with how the different bits of wavefront push against each other across the breadth of the channel. It's hard to talk about because the "ocean" through which the waves are propagating isn't in the kind of space we're used to -- it has, like, a googol of differently pointing dimensions (to cover all the different ways particles in the classical universe could be arranged), and the "channel" only keeps waves together along a few of those dimensions. (Also, said "channel" is made out of the abstract fact that some states of a quantum computing device would be higher-energy than others.)

Quantum computers can't breach into other parallel universes that have already fallen away from ours. And the distance required between two possible arrangements of particles, for the parts of the wavefront passing through those arrangements to get deflected away from each other into constituting clearly separate universes, is quite small -- it's what makes up the place where physics stops looking quantum and starts looking classical and familiar to common sense. But if you listen to the physicists, the phenomena used by quantum computing devices, that we might interpret as "parallel universes", are what has always already been going on at some level; it's just that our perspective was limited.

(Well, if you listen to physicists other than the ones who still agnostically stick to the Copenhagen interpretation, which is basically just a calculation rule for quantitatively how to sweep parallel universes under "collapse"-rugs once they've finished doing their job pushing the 'real', i.e. locally-remembered, universe into the appropriate place. Other physicists consider Copenhagen's supposedly-formal-but-actually-nonformal phenomenology of "collapses" to be explained away as hack-job approximations to the well-understood emergent phenomenon of decoherence.)

Obligatory occultism: On the other hand, there's some reason to suppose that quantum mechanics might be just a narrow special case of the process by which the inner planes settle on combinations of details for the outer planes. In that case, there'd potentially be enough intelligence going into that selection process that the process would be able to find a way for universes farther away from a focal "real" universe to not have the same uniform quantum mechanics, while still not detectably breaking anything about the regularities from which quantum mechanics emerges in the vicinity of that "real" universe. (This would be a form of the "mangled-worlds interpretation", in which parallel universes break down as soon as they become too relatively improbable.) What compels physicists towards the conclusion that the other parallel universes exist is a kind of Copernican anti-anthropocentrism: the premise that the laws of quantum mechanics are uniform even in parts of the wavefunction far from where we happen to find our cosmologically-unimportant selves. (That's my hypothesis, which is *ahem* somehow competing with the hypothesis that physicists are caused to believe in parallel universes by overexposure to science fiction.) If you take away the reason for quantum mechanics to be uniform, then maybe the parallel universes can meaningfully fail to exist after all.

comments.title: An interesting parallel?
comments.content:
So I'm reading Christophe Poncet's new book on the Tarot of Marseilles and finding it really interesting. In a nutshell, his theory is that the Marseilles tarot descends from a didactic card game created by Marsilio Ficino to teach the concepts behind Ficino's understanding of Platonism and Pythagoreanism.
This morning I was reading the first chapter of his thoughts on Trump 13, and he pointed out something I had noticed but never been able to puzzle out. He points out the small red "bulb" at the base of the skeleton's spine, and the fact that this appears to be plant-like in appearance, culminating in a small flower-like structure in the neck. This reminded me of the correlation JMG points out between the sex center vortex and the throat vortex in the Five Rites. Ficino commented on this, saying that the brain is the root and that a sap comes from it to the genitals, and in this he seems to be referencing a work wrongly attributed to Timaeus of Locri.
A further interesting thing that occurred to me about this is that Paul Foster Case (who uses a very Marseilles like design in his BOTA Tarot) says that there is a twist in the skeleton's spine that gives the whole secret to spiritual alchemy. I never had any idea of what he was talking about, but this makes some sense of it.
Is the hint here that, in non-awakened people, this energy flows down and out, but in the process of development this flow has to be turned around in the other direction? Could be...

replies.title: Re: An interesting parallel?
replies.content:
It's an interesting claim, though I wonder what evidence he provides. It's been tolerably well documented by European art historians that the tarot was invented sometime between 1411 and 1415 by Marziano da Tortona, the private secretary of the Duke of Milan. Some connection between Ficino and Marziano is certainly possible, but I'd want to see the receipts!

I can't speak to the Marseilles tarot, as I haven't studied it, but Case was very active in the American occult community at the time the Five Rites were being invented, and the private papers of the order he founded make it clear that he knew all about the same energy centers the Five Rites use.

replies.title: Re: An interesting parallel?
replies.content: Yes, when I first heard about this my immediate reaction was "Yeah, right...". Theories about the origins of the the Tarot and playing cards in general are abundant. But I have to confess that his book is very well researched and sourced, and I am becoming quite converted to his ideas. His starting point was the clothing depicted in the cards, which he says is Northern Italian, mid to late 15th century. Apparently there are other researchers who have said the same thing. He ISN'T claiming this is the origin of the Tarot, but rather the Tarot de Marseilles specifically, and he cites imagery from other Tarots that were earlier. In a related vein, there is also a tarocchi of Mantegna (which looks quite different from the standard Tarot deck) which has a similar didactic purpose claimed for it.

comments.title: Arthur Machen, weird fiction, and  fin de siècle science
comments.content:
Weird fiction and science at the Fin de siècle might be of some interest. Alder's basic idea is that that there was a current of "weirdness" in play in the late 19th century that manifested both in fiction (especially horror fantasy and early sf) and in the sciences, particularly physics and biology. This allows her to make some interesting connections, for example between HG Wells as a writer of popular science and as a writer of creepy fiction (for example, The Island of Doctor Moreau, which dealt with the theme of engineered bioplasticity about which he'd previously written).

Many writers of (what later were called) weird tales were arguably part of an ongoing conversation about the enweirdening, so to speak, of the world. One of her key authors is Arthur Machen, and she points out something interesting that Machen said, in a memoir, about his own "horror" fiction. Below is a passage extracted from the Project Gutenberg version of Far-Off Things (with page numbers helpfully included). It's a bit long, but it says something important about what Machen thought he was trying to do. The key bit begins "Here of course was my real failure", but the rest is necessary for context. I have put that part in bold rext.

The other writers dealt with are Robert Louis Stevenson, E. Nesbit E. and H. Heron, Algernon Blackwood, William Hope Hodgson, and H.G. Wells.

--LeGrand


I think that on the especial occasion that I have in mind the red row of houses must have been some portion or fragment of Harlesden. I remember that, like the cemetery, this impressed me as a wholly new and unforeseen horror, something as strange and terrible as the apparition of a rattlesnake or a boa-constrictor might be to an English child, wandering a little away into the orchard or the wood near the house. I had never lived in a world that might have prepared me for such things; in Gwent—in my day, at all events—there was no such phenomenon as this sudden and violent irruption of red brick in the midst of a green field; and thus when I came round the corner of a peaceful lane and saw in the midst of elms and meadows this staring spectacle, I was as aghast as Robinson[Pg 123] Crusoe when he saw the track of the foot on the sand of his desert island.
... And here I would make a parenthesis, and say that so long as my writing habits had any concern with the imagination I never departed from the one formula. This not consciously; in fact, I have a secret doctrine to the effect that in literature no imaginative effects are achieved by logical predetermination. I have told, I think, how I was confronted suddenly and for the first time with the awe and solemnity and mystery of the valley of the Usk, and of the house called Bartholly hanging solitary between the deep forest and the winding esses of the river. This spectacle remained in my heart for years, and at last I transliterated it, clumsily enough, in the story of "The Great God Pan," which, as a friendly critic once said, "does at least make one believe in the devil, if it does nothing else." Here, of course, was my real failure; I translated awe, at worst awfulness, into evil; again, I say, one dreams in fire and works in clay. But, at all events, my method never altered. More legitimately than in the instance of "The Great God Pan" I made the horrid apparition of the crude new houses in the midst of green pastures the seed of my tale, "The Inmost Light," which was originally bound up with "The Great God Pan." And so the man in my story, resting in green fields, looked up and saw a face that chilled his blood gazing at him from the back of one of those red houses that once had frightened me, when I was a sorry lad of twenty, wandering about the verges[Pg 124] of London. The doctor of my tale lived in Harlesden.

replies.title: Re: Arthur Machen, weird fiction, and  fin de siècle science
replies.content: Interesting. It's very odd that he would say that, since he was so insistent in his prose on the evilly evil evilness of the uncanny...

replies.title: Re: Arthur Machen, weird fiction, and  fin de siècle science
replies.content:
I suspect the "evilly evil" rhetoric was not exactly as it appears from 100 years later. I've just finished Hodgson's Carnacki stories -- quite disappointing, in some ways, though interesting enough. Very given to rather prosy descriptions of horrifying events and apparitions. It begins to seem to me that, all the way up through Lovecraft, there were writers who supposed that having characters talk about evil, horror, or whatnot, were adequate objective correlatives for portraying the experience of evil, horror, despair, and so on. (Nowadays it seems that emergency-room springloaded cat imagery is supposed to do the trick.)

By the way, a piece of patahistorical secret knowledge. Carnacki's father was one Tomasz Czarnacki who moved from the Cracow area to Paris, where he hobnobbed with other émigrés, including Józef Maria Hoene-Wroński. It was in this hotbed of esoteric and para-scientific experiment that he developed the early forms of the technology that he passed on to his son after he moved to Britain and in the process (like Joseph Conrad) anglicized his name. By the time Hodgson wrote about him, of course, his un-British origins were not evident.

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: Arthur Machen, weird fiction, and  fin de siècle science
replies.content: Hmm! I may have to drop that into Ariel Moravec's backstory somewhere. A character who's introduced in the third volume of her adventures, and who will be a continuing figure as we continue, is named Ladislaus Augustine Wronski-Bonaparte -- he goes by Austin Wronski -- and comes from that same Polish emigré scene in France. One of his ancestors, a cavalry officer in the French army who had an arm shot off in one of Napoleon III's Italian wars, married a distant and very homely Corsican cousin of that emperor, thus the hyphenated name. There may be some connection to the Czarnacki family in there somewhere...

comments.title: On Death and Dying (and Apologies to Kubler Ross)
comments.content:
Sorry if this question sounds insensitive, but it is not intended to be, and I am not clairvoyant and have no mystical powers that I am aware of.

But to those who do, why do you grieve if a loved one dies, as you (1) know where they are/where they are going (2) know that you will see them again in some future life and (3) may even be able to continue communicating with them now?

I am just trying to understand the psychology of this--I understand why non-psychics grieve, since they believe that the person has actually disappeared into nothingness; in this case, the death is a "loss". I just don't "get it" from the psychic perspective, since the death is, as best as I can tell, the evolution of a caterpillar to a butterfly (???).

replies.title: Re: On Death and Dying (and Apologies to Kubler Ross)
replies.content: I don't grieve for the dead person. My grief is a reflection of my own loneliness and loss; the other person, especially if it's someone who's been a constant companion and a source of strength and comfort, isn't present any more. It's as though they suddenly had to leave for a distant continent; you'd miss someone in that case, wouldn't you?

replies.title: Re: On Death and Dying (and Apologies to Kubler Ross)
replies.content:
I am sorry, but the truth is that I just really don't know, because I can't truly wrap my head around the fact that I am not my body and that there is a non-physical world. I can intellectualize it, rationalize it, and even emotionally argue for it, but I can't really relate to it in a fundamental sense.

I mean, if I lived in an absolute 3rd world slum/war zone and my significant other left for a distant continent that was a first world paradise, maybe I would just be really happy for them and look forward to the day I reunite with them?

In any case, I hope you get the companionship, strength and comfort you desire and need (and just to be clear, the original post was not directed at you personally!)

comments.title: Gods
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I just finished your book “A World Full of Gods” which I enjoyed.

I did have a question about the gods. It seems that the modern world has seen a big shift where gods are now known and worshipped outside of their usual setting. For instance, a Norseman wouldn’t usually have known about Ganesh unless he went to India. I, on the other hand, have a Hindu temple practically in my backyard, and you can see his image in any number of yoga studios across the country.

Is this just a consequence of modern communication, or are the gods consciously becoming less tribal and more universal?

replies.title: Re: Gods
replies.content: This happens in any mature civilization. In Roman times, for example, the Celtic horse goddess Epona became the patron goddess of the Roman cavalry, and so there are shrines to Epona in Egypt -- just as there were temples to Isis in Roman Britain. There were also Buddhist missionaries in Alexandria in 200 BC. We're just experiencing the normal mingling of the gods that happens once a civilization passes its peak.
